content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
March 14, 1989 
Honorable Curt Pringle 
Assemblyman, Seventy-Second District 
state Capitol 
P.o. Box 942849 
Sacramento, CA 
94249-0001 
Dear Assemblyman Pringle: 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-89-155 
E 
You have requested confirmation of advice under the Political 
Reform Act (the "Act").11 
As I informed you in our telephone 
conversation of March 10, 1989, we have reviewed your request and 
we confirm that you may raise contributions in excess of the 
limits set forth in sections 85301, 85302, 85303 and 85305 to 
defend against the recall effort. 
For purposes of these statutes, 
we consider the recall campaign to be a campaign to support or 
oppose a ball~t measure, and thus exempt from the contribution 
limits. 
(See section 82043.) 
To raise funds to oppose the recall effort, you are required 
to establish a committee that is separate from your other 
controlled committees. 
No funds collected by this committee may 
be used for your other election campaign or officeholder expenses. 
I also advised you that your name is required to be included in 
the name of this committee. 
(Section 84106.) 
Finally, I informed you that we cannot confirm your 
understanding about the use of funds raised by other candidates, 
political committees and political parties prior to January 1, 
1989 to support or oppose the recall campaign against you. 
I 
advised you that Regulation 18536.2 (copy enclosed) expressly 
prohibits other candidates, political committees and political 
parties from using any funds raised prior to January 1, 1989 to 
II Government Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations 
section 18000, et seq. 
All references to regulations are to 
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


Honorable Curt Pringle 
March 14, 1989 
Page 2 
support or oppose the recall effort against you. 
(Regulation 
18536.2 (b) (3) and (c) (1).) 
If you have any questions, please contact me at 
(916) 322-5901. 
DMG:KED:plh 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
\km~f/.~/~ 
By: 
Kaertryn E. Donovan 
Counsel, Legal Division 


STAT~ CAPITOL 
• PO BOX 942849 
CA 94249·0001 
(91 
445·7333 
Mr. John Larson 
Chairman 
March 9, 1989 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
428 J Street, Suite 800 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Dear Mr. Larson: 
1 
DISTRICT 
COMMITTEES 
REVENUE 
TAXATION 
LOCAL GOVERNMENT 
ELECTIONS. REAPPORTIONMENT 
& CONSTITUTIONAL AMENDMENTS 
I would greatly appreciate confirmation from you regarding 
Proposition 73 and its effect on recall elections. 
It is my understanding that the FPPC is advising proponents 
and opponents (specifically an elected official who is the subject 
of the recall) that the Proposition 73 contribution limits do not 
apply to recall elections. 
Thus, both proponents and opponents 
are able to raise funds in unlimited amounts. 
It is my further understanding that the elected official who 
is the object of a recall campaign is able to simultaneously raise 
funds in unlimited amounts for the recall election and also raise 
funds under Proposition 73 contribution limits for his re-election 
campaign, provided he has filed his 501 Statement of Intent. 
Moreover, the contributions an elected official receives for a 
recall campaign would not be cumulated. 
I am faced with an 
announced recall and, at least, one $10,000 contribution has been 
donated to that recall effort. 
On the basis of the foregoing, I 
understand that: (1) I can now begin to raise the unlimited funds 
to defend myself in the recall campaign; (2) Restricted and 1988 
funds from a candidate, political committee, and\or political 
party may be contributed to me as well as the proponent and 
opponents of the recall campaign. 


Thank you for your assistance. 
I would appreciate hearing 
from you as soon as possible since this is such a pressing matter. 
CURT PRING 
cc: 
Assemblyman Ross Johnson

sections.question: It is my understanding that the FPPC is advising proponents and opponents (specifically an elected official who is the subject of the recall) that the Proposition 73 contribution limits do not apply to recall elections. Thus, both proponents and opponents are able to raise funds in unlimited amounts. It is my further understanding that the elected official who is the object of a recall campaign is able to simultaneously raise funds in unlimited amounts for the recall election and also raise funds under Proposition 73 contribution limits for his re-election campaign, provided he has filed his 501 Statement of Intent. Moreover, the contributions an elected official receives for a recall campaign would not be cumulated. I am faced with an announced recall and, at least, one $10,000 contribution has been donated to that recall effort. On the basis of the foregoing, I understand that: (1) I can now begin to raise the unlimited funds to defend myself in the recall campaign; (2) Restricted and 1988 funds from a candidate, political committee, and/or political party may be contributed to me as well as the proponent and opponents of the recall campaign.
sections.conclusion: We have reviewed your request and we confirm that you may raise contributions in excess of the limits set forth in sections 85301, 85302, 85303 and 85305 to defend against the recall effort. For purposes of these statutes, we consider the recall campaign to be a campaign to support or oppose a ballot measure, and thus exempt from the contribution limits. To raise funds to oppose the recall effort, you are required to establish a committee that is separate from your other controlled committees. No funds collected by this committee may be used for your other election campaign or officeholder expenses. Your name is required to be included in the name of this committee. We cannot confirm your understanding about the use of funds raised by other candidates, political committees and political parties prior to January 1, 1989 to support or oppose the recall campaign against you. Regulation 18536.2 expressly prohibits other candidates, political committees and political parties from using any funds raised prior to January 1, 1989 to support or oppose the recall effort against you.
sections.question_synthetic: Can an elected official subject to a recall campaign raise contributions in excess of Proposition 73 contribution limits to defend against the recall, and can restricted and prior-year funds from other candidates and committees be used to support or oppose the recall effort?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that unlimited contributions may be raised to defend against a recall campaign through a separate committee bearing the candidate's name, with no cross-use to other campaigns. However, the FPPC cannot confirm that pre-January 1, 1989 funds from other candidates, committees, and parties can be used to support or oppose the recall, and cites Regulation 18536.2 as expressly prohibiting such use.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (dated March 9, 1989) and FPPC response (dated March 14, 1989). The response clearly identifies this as an advice letter on contribution limits and recall campaign financing. File No. A-89-155.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: It is my understanding that the FPPC is advising proponents and opponents (specifically an elected official who is the subject of the recall) that the Proposition 73 contribution limits do not apply to recall elections. Thus, both proponents and opponents are able to raise funds in unlimited amounts. It is my further understanding that the elected official who is the object of a recall campaign is able to simultaneously raise funds in unlimited amounts for the recall election and also raise funds under Proposition 73 contribution limits for his re-election campaign, provided he has filed his 501 Statement of Intent. Moreover, the contributions an elected official receives for a recall campaign would not be cumulated. I am faced with an announced recall and, at least, one $10,000 contribution has been donated to that recall effort. On the basis of the foregoing, I understand that: (1) I can now begin to raise the unlimited funds to defend myself in the recall campaign; (2) Restricted and 1988 funds from a candidate, political committee, and/or political party may be contributed to me as well as the proponent and opponents of the recall campaign.

CONCLUSION: We have reviewed your request and we confirm that you may raise contributions in excess of the limits set forth in sections 85301, 85302, 85303 and 85305 to defend against the recall effort. For purposes of these statutes, we consider the recall campaign to be a campaign to support or oppose a ballot measure, and thus exempt from the contribution limits. To raise funds to oppose the recall effort, you are required to establish a committee that is separate from your other controlled committees. No funds collected by this committee may be used for your other election campaign or officeholder expenses. Your name is required to be included in the name of this committee. We cannot confirm your understanding about the use of funds raised by other candidates, political committees and political parties prior to January 1, 1989 to support or oppose the recall campaign against you. Regulation 18536.2 expressly prohibits other candidates, political committees and political parties from using any funds raised prior to January 1, 1989 to support or oppose the recall effort against you.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises Assemblyman Curt Pringle that he may raise contributions in excess of Proposition 73 limits to defend against a recall campaign by establishing a separate committee, but confirms that pre-1989 restricted funds from other entities cannot be used for the recall effort.
